Learning Objective 39C

Major Landmarks of the Skull

Identify the bones, sutures, and foramina of the skull as seen in dried skulls and CT images.

Contents

1Bones of the Cranium 2Sutures & Craniometric Points 3Foramina & Cranial Fossae 4CT Imaging Correlation 5MCQ (10 Questions)

1. Bones of the Cranium

The cranium consists of 22 named bones divided into the neurocranium (8 bones forming the brain case) and the viscerocranium (14 bones forming the facial skeleton). On a dried skull, you should be able to identify each bone and its key features from multiple viewing angles.

Lateral View

The lateral aspect shows contributions from both neurocranium and viscerocranium. Key identifiable bones: frontal, parietal, temporal (squamous and mastoid parts), occipital, sphenoid (greater wing), zygomatic, maxilla, nasal, lacrimal, and mandible. The temporal fossa, zygomatic arch, external acoustic meatus, and mastoid process are key landmarks.

Fig 8.4A — Bones of lateral aspect (color-coded). Frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital, sphenoid (greater wing), zygomatic, maxilla, nasal, lacrimal, mandible. Note the pterion at the junction of four bones.

Anterior View

Dominated by the viscerocranium. The frontal bone forms the upper frame (forehead + supra-orbital margins). Key features: supra-orbital foramen/notch, glabella, superciliary arches, orbital cavities, piriform aperture (nasal opening), infra-orbital foramen, and mental foramen. These three foramina lie in an approximately vertical line — high yield for identification.

Fig 8.3 — Anterior (facial) aspect, color-coded. Bones: frontal, sphenoid, temporal, parietal, nasal, lacrimal, zygomatic, maxilla, mandible, ethmoid, vomer, inferior concha.

Superior View

The calvaria from above shows three bones and three sutures. The frontal bone anteriorly, paired parietal bones forming the bulk, and occipital bone posteriorly. Bregma (coronal × sagittal sutures) and lambda (sagittal × lambdoid sutures) are identifiable landmarks. The parietal eminence marks the widest part of the skull.

Fig 8.8 — Superior aspect. (A) Color-coded calvaria with coronal, sagittal, and lambdoid sutures. (B) Bregma (vertex is the topmost point). (C) Lambda, parietal foramen, posterosuperior view.

Posterior View

The occiput is dominated by the squamous occipital bone with its external occipital protuberance (inion), superior nuchal line, and the external occipital crest. Parietal bones contribute superiorly, and mastoid parts of the temporal bone are visible laterally. Lambda marks where the sagittal and lambdoid sutures converge.

Fig 8.7 — Occipital (posterior) aspect. (A) Occiput with bones and landmarks. Sagittal and lambdoid sutures meet at lambda. (B) Posterior cranial fossa — squamous occipital removed showing internal features.

Inferior View (External Cranial Base)

The most complex view. The foramen magnum sits centrally in the occipital bone, flanked by the occipital condyles. Anteriorly: the hard palate (maxillae + palatine bones), choanae, vomer. The sphenoid contributes the pterygoid processes and greater wing. The temporal bone shows the mandibular fossa, styloid process, mastoid process, and petrous part.

Fig 8.9A — External cranial base (color-coded). Contributing bones: frontal, maxilla, parietal, sphenoid, temporal, occipital, palatine, vomer, zygomatic.
Fig 8.9B — External cranial base features. Key landmarks: foramen magnum, occipital condyles, jugular foramen, carotid canal, foramen ovale, foramen spinosum, mandibular fossa, stylomastoid foramen, mastoid process, incisive fossa, choanae.

2. Sutures & Craniometric Points

Sutures are fibrous joints that unite the flat bones of the calvaria. On dried skulls they appear as interlocking, serrated lines. On CT, sutures can mimic fracture lines — knowing their locations is essential.

Major Sutures

SutureBones ConnectedVisible From
CoronalFrontal ↔ ParietalSuperior, lateral
SagittalR Parietal ↔ L ParietalSuperior
LambdoidParietal / Temporal ↔ OccipitalSuperior, posterior
SquamousTemporal ↔ ParietalLateral
Frontal (Metopic)R ↔ L FrontalAnterior, superior (~8% adults)

Craniometric Points

PointLocationClinical Relevance
PterionJunction of sphenoid, squamous temporal, frontal, parietalThinnest calvaria; overlies middle meningeal a. → epidural hematoma risk
BregmaCoronal × sagittal suture junctionAnterior fontanelle site (neonatal)
LambdaSagittal × lambdoid suture junctionPosterior fontanelle site (neonatal)
VertexTopmost point of neurocraniumNear midpoint of sagittal suture
AsterionJunction of parietomastoid, occipitomastoid, lambdoid suturesOverlies junction of transverse & sigmoid sinuses
GlabellaMidline prominence on frontal boneTap reflex; radiographic landmark
InionPeak of external occipital protuberanceApproximates confluence of sinuses internally
NasionFrontonasal × internasal suture junctionRoot of nose; radiographic landmark
Fig 8.6 — Craniometric points, lateral view. Vertex, bregma, pterion, lambda, glabella, nasion, inion, and asterion identified on the skull.
Clinical Pearl — Sutures vs. Fractures on CT
Sutures are bilateral, symmetric, have sclerotic (white) margins, and follow known anatomical paths. Fractures are irregular, unilateral, lack sclerotic margins, and do not follow suture lines. Wormian (sutural) bones along the lambdoid suture are normal variants — not fragments.

3. Foramina & Cranial Fossae

The internal cranial base has three stepped fossae — anterior (shallowest), middle, and posterior (deepest) — each containing specific foramina. Identifying these on dried skulls and correlating with CT is essential.

Fig 8.12A — Internal cranial base (color-coded). Frontal, ethmoid, sphenoid, temporal, parietal, occipital. All foramina labeled with their positions in the anterior, middle, and posterior fossae.
Fig 8.12B — Cranial fossae (superolateral view). Anterior (green), middle (orange), posterior (purple) fossae. Note how they step down from front to back.

Anterior Cranial Fossa

Formed by: orbital parts of frontal bone, cribriform plate of ethmoid, lesser wings + body of sphenoid. Houses the frontal lobes.

Foramen / ApertureContents
Foramen cecumNasal emissary vein (inconstant)
Cribriform foramina (in cribriform plate)Olfactory nerve (CN I) axons
Anterior & posterior ethmoidal foraminaEthmoidal vessels and nerves

Middle Cranial Fossa

Formed by: body + greater wings of sphenoid, squamous temporal bones laterally. Houses the temporal lobes and pituitary gland (in sella turcica).

Foramen / ApertureContents
Optic canalCN II (optic nerve), ophthalmic artery
Superior orbital fissureCN III, IV, V₁, VI; ophthalmic veins; sympathetic fibers
Foramen rotundumCN V₂ (maxillary nerve)
Foramen ovaleCN V₃ (mandibular nerve), accessory meningeal a.
Foramen spinosumMiddle meningeal a. & v., meningeal branch of CN V₃
Foramen lacerumDeep petrosal n., meningeal arterial branches (ICA passes across, not through)

Posterior Cranial Fossa

Formed by: occipital bone, petrous + mastoid parts of temporal bone, dorsum sellae of sphenoid. Houses the cerebellum, pons, and medulla.

Foramen / ApertureContents
Foramen magnumMedulla, meninges, vertebral aa., CN XI, spinal aa.
Jugular foramenCN IX, X, XI; IJV; inferior petrosal + sigmoid sinuses
Hypoglossal canalCN XII (hypoglossal nerve)
Internal acoustic meatusCN VII, CN VIII; labyrinthine artery
Condylar canalEmissary vein (sigmoid sinus → vertebral veins)
Mastoid foramenMastoid emissary vein, meningeal branch of occipital a.
Fig 8.11 — Cranial foramina. Superior (internal) and inferior (external) views of the cranial base. Color-coded bones with all major foramina labeled. Compare internal vs. external views to correlate each foramen's location.

4. CT Imaging Correlation

CT images of the skull allow you to identify the same bones, sutures, and foramina seen on dried skulls — but in cross-section (axial, coronal, sagittal planes). On CT, dense cortical bone appears bright white, cancellous bone (diploë) appears gray, air appears black, and sutures appear as dark lines with sclerotic (white) borders.

What to Identify on Head CT

As you scroll through the axial slices, correlate with the dried skull landmarks:

Bones: frontal, parietal, temporal (squamous, petrous), occipital, sphenoid (greater wing, body/sella), ethmoid (cribriform plate). Sutures: coronal, sagittal, lambdoid, squamous — appear as symmetric lucent lines with white margins. Foramina: foramen magnum, jugular foramen, carotid canal, foramen ovale, foramen spinosum appear as well-defined dark openings in bone on axial CT.

Also note: the frontal and sphenoidal sinuses appear as black (air-filled) spaces within the bones, the mastoid air cells are visible posterior to the petrous temporal, and the sella turcica is identifiable on sagittal and coronal reconstructions.

🧠
Open Willard Head CT Viewer →
UNE Brightspace · Anatomy Core Site · Head CT001
Scroll through axial slices to identify bones, sutures, and foramina in cross-section.
Tip for CT Identification
Start at the vertex and scroll inferiorly. First you'll see the calvaria (frontal, parietal, occipital bones and sagittal suture). As you descend, the temporal squama and sphenoid greater wing appear. Further down, the petrous temporal, sella turcica, and cranial base foramina become visible. The foramen magnum is the last major landmark before transitioning to cervical spine.
